item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The images used in this article are non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license. The first image comes from here and is All Rights Reserved. The second image comes from this video and has not been released under Creative Commons. Thus, the images have been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know it's been addressed beefore, but this first picture does not depict bees, but yellow jackets.
Even though it's an article from 2015, wouldn't it be right to try to find an image that fits better? Just for the sake of rightness (and, well, it wouldn't be the first time an old image is removed).

item.title: Too interesting, sorry for necroposting
item.content:encoded:
I loved this skip precisely because it isn't really just body-horror. It makes me wonder how much of the resulting entity is 2401, how much is Mary.

But though it already got my upvote, I have some issues with it.

The problem is that the article makes it too obvious that the "monster" isn't 2401, but Alpha. There is a lot of text dedicated to Mary both before and after transformation, Alpha is the focus of containment procedures when it should be just a byproduct of research, and there is too much of mystery about a D-Class instead of the anomaly.

As much as I liked how the horror here is in the relationship between Mary and Lamb, and how that relationship becomes virulent, the article could work better by letting it be tangential. I want more information on 2401 proper, I want less mystery on Mary's past either by showing it or by not making it such a big deal.

Oh, and less fatality rates for the subjects would be nice. It doesn't really improve it to have only one successful colony. Other subjects have survived more stressful situations without such a high rate of suicide, and the Foundation have better management for those who try. Mary can be a special case without being the single one, and we could even have some other examples of successful colonies to see how 2401 changes behavior based on the original host.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Somehow I didn't comment about this before over here, probably because I was too grossed out, but… Seeing my name here isn't doing me too good man.

It doesn't help when I have a brother called Kenneth as well.

+1

item.title: JUNIOR STAFF POST OPEN - Crosslink Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer expired, edits implemented.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team.

+ Proposed Edits
- Proposed Edits

A crosslink is being proposed to Research Site-45 as it is referenced in SCP-2401's containment procedures, and the dossier page for Research Site-45 links back to SCP-2401. In addition, the author of SCP-2401 is also the author of the dossier page for Research Site-45.

Editing this:

SCP-2401 and SCP-2401-Alpha are to be stored together in Cell-22 at Research Site-45.

To this:

SCP-2401 and SCP-2401-Alpha are to be stored together in Cell-22 at Research Site-45.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yep, those are yellowjackets.

item.title: Please read in context
item.content:encoded:
Seriously, this is a VERY sensitive topic
– hide block

I think what really makes this work is how, to a large degree, this is the most realistic depiction of rape we have on the site. In horror, and in this very site, rape (and other forms of sexual assault) usually get presented by highlighting either how evil and/or inhuman the perpetrator is, the distress of the victim (often emphasizing how "undeserving" they are to such a calamity1), and/or the brutality of the act itself.

That isn't bad in itself, but it straddles over many of the parts that make sexual assault so damaging in reality. There are monsters like Richard Ramirez or Jeffrey Dahmer out there, but they are statistically insignificant. Most cases of sexual assault are committed by people the victims knew, and many times in situations where the victim may end up with some self-doubt about whether or not they could have prevented it had they acted differently2. The act itself is traumatic, but it's the aftermath which compounds it over other several forms of damage.

Another element that makes this more interesting is the background of Mary. One can't help but wonder how much her life experiences led to the event. Was it just a humanoid monster luring her prey into a trap, or was this a conscious decision from a person (who was broken beforehand by her own traumatic experiences)?

This is, in my opinion, a very well crafted article on a very difficult subject.

Footnotes
1. Just to clarify, I am not saying anyone somehow deserves this, just that this is a common element in narratives
2. Again, please don't misconstrue this as me blaming any victim, just observing something that pops up.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've noticed that insects on first picture aren't bees. I reverse searched that picture.

God, what have I done. I want to unsee that.

item.title: Re: Euclid?
item.content:encoded: Why's that?

item.title: Euclid?
item.content:encoded: I don't know, but… I think this one could be Keter.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I love it but the first pic looks too much like a watermelon.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lol, didn't mean to ruin your Thanksgiving if that's the case.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Jesus Christ, I just completely lost my appetite.

Take my +1, you monster.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's not the rape aspects that bother me here, those were handled pretty much as well as they could be. It's the poor dialogue and characterization that falls flat. It's trying to get me invested in characters and a situation without actually doing the work for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's easy to get so caught up in being politically correct that we lose sight of context. To me, this is a horrifying swarm of SYMBIOTIC BEES AAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAAA.

Didn't think I'd end up upvoting Weird Sex Stuff, but I can't really see bee woman doing the mimic thing so much as as an extremely unnerving SCP.

To clarify, it doesn't strike me as there for the weird fetishism value like many of the others do. It was written like that because it is horrifying.

item.title: I joke
item.content:encoded: Evidence confirming that SCP-2401 and the bees are working with the president

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'd agree. I think female on male rape is usually played for laughs or simply considered not traumatic, so that's what I expected. But I was surprised to see it got the seriousness it deserved and overall, I feel it enhanced the story.

The rest of the article was good, as well. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yeah, once I read the part about 2401 being bees I immediately looked back, since I was certain that I'd seen a picture of yellow jackets apparently burrowing into someone's skin and definitely not bees. Since I'm guessing (hoping) that that is not a real picture, surely a similar picture of bees could have been spliced in instead. If that picture is, God forbid, actually real…there is not enough wasp spray in the world for me to sleep tonight.

EDIT: Having found the original image via reverse image search, I must congratulate daveyoufool (or whoever made the image for the article) on a squicky job well done on a great starting point for the intended end result (though they still aren't bees). I couldn't get the page it is supposed to be on to load properly, so I don't know exactly what medium the wasps are digging into in the original.

I have been a bit more enlightened as to what I am guessing the inspiration behind this SCP is, however.
Author can confirm or deny if they so desire.

Trypophobia

Original image URL
If anyone else can tell what it is the wasps are in/on.

http://pthumbnails.5min.com/10359100/517954978_cv1_o.jpg

Page it is supposedly on, if it will load for anyone else.
I get no pictures, video, or comments loading.

http://on.aol.com/video/what-is-trypophobia--517954978

Incidentally, I do NOT recommend duplicating the reverse image search I did if you do not want copious amounts of squick flooding your screen.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This isn't a factor in my vote, but that first photo, despite being effective body horror, is very conspicuously not of bees.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ Open
- Close

Let me just first say that I appreciate your concern in this reply. This was an issue that made me very hesitant in posting so I took steps to ensure that this was written as tactfully as possible. That said, I'm going to try and address some of your concerns with this piece without turning into a discussion on social issues. The rape was not inserted solely for its shock value, far from it. Many people call this a body horror skip. The body horror is a ruse. It's to lead you in to thinking what is obviously going on, then peel back the layers to show a more sinister intent.

I respectfully disagree with your statement, mainly because I was aiming for a very specific kind of horror. The kind of horror when your trust and complacency turns on you when the other party uses sociopathic methods to achieve a deviant objective. This to me can be a very terrifying prospect and it makes me particularly paranoid with some people in my life. Who are you really? What do you want really? Can I trust to be alone with you? I believe I left plenty of breadcrumbs beforehand to lead up to this. It's not suddenly BOOM RAPEY TIME!!!

Still, I can stand here and justify this all I want. At the end of the day, it will still be intolerable for folks to read, and that's fine. I knew that going in. This skip covers a very dark and heavy subject that's just going to trigger a negative response regardless. You mention this as disrespectful, but it is a common theme in lots of other literature as well… so is kidnapping, murder, robbery, war, torture, and other heinous acts. But that still doesn't make it any less intolerable for people. So I can respect your's and other's sentiment.

As for the zoology bit, I leave that for the reader to interpret on their own. It's a bee mind in a human body… wires are going to get crossed.

TL;DR: I appreciate the concern, but it's not in there for shock value. I also realize that this is an intolerable and sensitive subject for a lot of folks, so I respect their position as well. You have every right to hate it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You might be thinking of SCP-1702

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thank you Scantron for articulating what I was unable to.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For me, it's not a matter of whether the rape was handled tastefully. If this were talking about some already-given event that you were describing, then it would be. But this article sprung entirely from the author's imagination, and as such, I'll judge its inclusion and placement as well.

The body horror potential of bees making hives out of humans is very high, and a lot of this article's upvotes are riding off of it. Thing is, though, you kind of brush over a lot of the potentially good-nasty stuff, like what exactly infection entails, the epidemiology, the particular symptoms, etc.. When I skip over things in my writing, it's because I don't think they're important; I can only assign the same motivation to the author. In lieu of this, it seems, most of the article's attention is paid to developing the relationship between Kenneth and Mary, and describing the incident and its aftermath.

As for the incident itself: if there must be rape in an article, this is more or less how it should be handled. But there doesn't have to be rape in this article. It's a body horror premise — adding rape is either deviating from that premise for no good reason or intended as a continuation of that premise. You're a competent author, so I'm assuming it's the latter. It's using the spectacle of the act (I don't think I need to go on about why rape is a big deal here, but pretend I did) for… what, exactly? It's disrespectful.

And on another note, it makes no sense to me for the mating and reproduction organization of bees to line up at all with human conceptions of gender and sex. The roles and properties assigned to each by society/the hive don't mesh intuitively. The genetics are not analogous. Basically the birthing of young is, like, the solitary shared thing. I don't believe that the "human male:human female::bee male:bee female" dynamic would arise naturally, or even be designed artificially with any attention paid to the reality of the situation. It only really makes sense if it was made artificially and A) those involved don't know how bees work (unlikely), or B) the creators wanted these dynamics specifically, and thus probably knew that it would end up in a woman extracting reproductive material from a man. Probably in-universe that was incidental, but in real life, knowing that it ends up being the thrust of the article, it's bad zoology for the sake of a bad punchline.

tl;dr You went out of your way, away from a good article, to write about a rape. That's just as obnoxious as handling rape poorly, and IMO kind of worse. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Grade AAA body horror here. +1

A Foundation with a humanity factor is also my favourite kind of Foundation.

I initially thought I'd clicked the wrong mainlist link and had to double-check that "Mary Had a Little Lamb" really involved those pictures. But now I see exactly what you did there and kind of regret it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Two points:

Colonization does differ slightly in female hosts, whereas there is a much lesser risk of infection

I'm pretty sure that "whereas" would be more grammatically correct just be "as", here. Not one hundred percent sure, though.

It has been difficult to requisition female D-class because of their scarcity.

This line caught me offguard, largely because I don't see why. Probably just because I don't subscribe to the death row theory, and because there are plenty of other articles with female D-class. It's not really a necessary line in my opinion anyway.

On the whole, this was well-written, and I feel the rather touchy subject brought up was handled tastefully. The pictures were good too, although my hatred of insects didn't let me inspect too well… However, at its heart, this was just a body horror skip, and frankly, I'm not keen on those sorts of articles. All the same, upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You had me at the first picture. By which I mean I shuddered, upvoted in case failing to do so released bees, and scrolled the hell down to read in peace.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I changed "she much better" to "she feels much better" in Lamb's log. Please feel free to revert if I did anything wrong.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Goes without saying that I love this.

It's creepy and makes my skin feel weird.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the rape distinctly works, for the record. (I hope I never have to type that sentence again.)

That said, while this is great body horror and I like how far you extended everything with the interviews, I felt like there were some niggling tone issues. There's also another article, I swear, about a woman becoming the queen of some kind of anomalous insect colony, just with less body horror.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Not the bees…

Anything but the bees…

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well. Consider me officially squicked out. I suspect the rape is going to get a lot of discussion, though. For my part, I can see the relevance. From a human point of view, it was exactly that. LurkD handled it well, though, keeping everything clinical and avoiding a more graphic depiction that probably would have been less acceptable. I can also see it from 2401's perspective, though, and that's the part that horrifies me. It wasn't a woman raping a man, it was a queen mating with a drone. The final interview makes it pretty clear that it's no longer human and hasn't been for some time. Just because it was able to act that way to lure Lamb into a false sense of safety does not make it less of a monster. It behaved as a queen bee does, limited by its humanoid biology.

Edit: That first image still makes me cringe.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wasn't sure how I felt either, but both the act and the aftermath are being treated with enough respect that I wasn't immediately turned away by its inclusion. I don't see a problem with it being present in the SCP, especially since that's a major part of the concept relating to it, thanks to the care that went into its incorporation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope, nope, nope, can't do it, too gross. Nope, nope, nope.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, I am not sure how I feel about the rape here.

item.title: Special Thanks
item.content:encoded:
Thank you to all the people who helped me temper this idea of mine. Special thanks to DrKens for helping me doctor that photo at the top.

Enjoy.

Folks, here is a very good production of SCP-2401 created by Synthetic Alien, enjoy:

LINK

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+ 5/2401 clearance required
- OH GOD NOT THE BEES

AAAAAAGH THEY'RE IN MY EYES
